content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 15 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 107r–108v | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_015_page_001.jpg, DEU_015_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 153

---

1 At the end of every seven years you shall grant a release.

2 And this is the manner of the release: every creditor shall release what he has lent to his neighbor. He shall not exact it of his neighbor and his brother, because the LORD's release has been proclaimed.

3 Of a foreigner you may exact it, but whatever of yours is with your brother your hand shall release.

4 But there will be no poor among you — for the LORD will surely bless you in the land that the LORD your God is giving you for an inheritance to possess —

5 if only you fully obey the voice of the LORD your God, being careful to do all this commandment that I am commanding you today.

6 For the LORD your God will bless you, as he promised you, and you shall lend to many nations, but you shall not borrow, and you shall rule over many nations, but they shall not rule over you.

7 If among you there is a poor man — one of your brothers — within any of your gates in your land that the LORD your God is giving you, you shall not harden your heart and you shall not shut your hand against your poor brother.

8 But you shall surely open your hand to him and shall surely lend him sufficient for his need, whatever he lacks.

9 Take care lest there be a worthless thought in your heart, saying: "The seventh year, the year of release, is near," so that your eye is hostile toward your poor brother and you give him nothing, and he cries to the LORD against you, and you bear sin.

10 You shall surely give to him, and your heart shall not be grieved when you give to him, because for this thing the LORD your God will bless you in all your work and in all that you put your hand to.

11 For the poor will never cease from the land. Therefore I am commanding you: you shall surely open your hand to your brother, to the needy and to the poor in your land.

12 If your brother, a Hebrew man or a Hebrew woman, is sold to you, he shall serve you for six years, and in the seventh year you shall set him free from you.

13 And when you set him free from you, you shall not send him away empty-handed.

14 You shall furnish him liberally out of your flock, out of your threshing floor, and out of your winepress. As the LORD your God has blessed you, you shall give to him.

15 You shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt, and the LORD your God redeemed you; therefore I am commanding you this thing today.

16 But if he says to you: "I will not go out from you," because he loves you and your household, since he is well-off with you,

17 then you shall take an awl and thrust it through his ear into the door, and he shall be your slave forever. And you shall do the same to your female slave.

18 It shall not seem hard to you when you set him free, for at half the cost of a hired worker he has served you six years. And the LORD your God will bless you in all that you do.

19 All the firstborn males that are born of your herd and flock you shall consecrate to the LORD your God. You shall not work with the firstborn of your oxen, and you shall not shear the firstborn of your flock.

20 You shall eat it before the LORD your God year by year, in the place that the LORD will choose, you and your household.

21 But if it has any blemish — if it is lame or blind, or has any serious blemish — you shall not sacrifice it to the LORD your God.

22 You shall eat it within your gates. The unclean and the clean alike may eat it, as they would eat the gazelle or the deer.

23 Only you shall not eat its blood; you shall pour it out on the ground like water.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Verse 1 — shemittah (release):** "Release" for *shemittah* (lit. "letting fall/dropping"). The seventh-year remission of debts. The LXX renders *aphesin* ("release/forgiveness") — the same word used for forgiveness of sins in the NT, perhaps not coincidentally given the Jubilee trajectory through prophetic and gospel tradition.

**Verse 2 — ba'al masheh yado (the creditor/master of the loan of his hand):** "Every creditor" renders *ba'al masheh yado* (lit. "master of the lending of his hand"). The phrase is formal and legal. LXX: *ho echon ti en cheiri tou plesion* ("whoever has something in the hand of his neighbor").

**Verse 2 — shemittat YHWH (the LORD's release):** "The LORD's release has been proclaimed" for *qara shemittah la-YHWH*. The release is not merely socioeconomic but theological — it is the LORD's own release. LXX: *aphesis kyriou* — which echoes the LXX rendering of the Jubilee language.

**Verse 4 — efes ki (but/for there will be no):** The tension between v. 4 ("there will be no poor among you") and v. 11 ("the poor will never cease from the land") is intentional. Verse 4 is the covenantal ideal; verse 11 acknowledges the continuing reality that requires ongoing generosity. LOGOS preserves both without harmonization.

**Verse 9 — beliya'al (worthless/base):** "Worthless thought" for *davar beliya'al* (lit. "a word/thing of beliya'al"). The same root as *benei beliya'al* ("worthless men") in ch. 13:13. Here used for an internal temptation toward stinginess before the shemittah year. LXX: *rhema en te kardia sou anomia* ("a word of lawlessness in your heart").

**Verse 11 — patoach tiftach et yadekha (you shall surely open your hand):** Emphatic infinitive absolute construction: "you shall surely open your hand." The same construction appears at v. 8 for lending. The doubling of the verb is a Hebrew rhetorical device indicating moral urgency; rendered "surely" in English following established convention.

**Verse 12 — eved ivri (Hebrew slave):** "Hebrew man" and "Hebrew woman" for *ish ivri* and *ishah ivrit*. The term *ivri* (Hebrew) here likely designates a social/ethnic category. The law parallels Exod 21:2–6 but includes women explicitly, which is an expansion in Deuteronomy. LXX: *adelphon sou ton Hebraion* ("your brother the Hebrew").

**Verse 17 — martzea' (awl):** "Awl" for *martzea'* — a pointed tool used to pierce the ear. The door-piercing ritual marked permanent, voluntary servitude. LXX: *to opetirion* — a boring instrument.

**Verse 17 — ve-haya lekha eved 'olam (he shall be your slave forever):** "Forever" for *'olam* — in context meaning "for life" or "permanently." LXX: *doulos aionios* ("a slave for eternity/age").

**Verse 19 — taqdish (consecrate):** "You shall consecrate" for *taqdish* (Hiphil of *qadash*, to make holy). The firstborn belong to YHWH by covenant right (cf. Exod 13:2, 12). LXX: *hagiaseis* — exact equivalent.

**Verse 21 — mum (blemish):** "Blemish" for *mum* — the sacrificial disqualifying defect. The law on blemished animals links the firstborn offering to the altar standards of Leviticus. LXX: *momos* — transliterated into Greek.

*— LOGOS*
